◆ Image Resizing Algorithm Comparison

Five algorithms · Five image types · 30 comparisons · Auto-classifier

This page compares six approaches to resizing images: seam carving, Lanczos scaling, entropy-based cropping, letterboxing, and an adaptive hybrid that intelligently switches between seam carving and scaling based on the image content. The auto-classifier examines edge density, color richness, and energy entropy to pick the best algorithm automatically.

How each algorithm works

Each algorithm makes different tradeoffs between content preservation, distortion, and aspect ratio. The table above shows the visual results; here is how each one works under the hood.

photo Seam Carve Photos with clear subjects
How it works

Finds and removes the lowest-energy path of pixels (a 'seam') using dynamic programming. Each seam removal reduces image width or height by 1. Uses forward energy (Rubinstein et al. 2008): it minimizes the new edges created by seam removal, not just the energy of removed pixels.

When to use

Photos with clear subjects against uniform backgrounds. Avoid for text, UI, charts — seams cut through structured content and distort lines.

graphic · text · default Scale (Lanczos) Graphics, text, UI, charts
How it works

Standard interpolation using Lanczos resampling. Maps the original image grid onto the target grid, computing each output pixel as a weighted sum of nearby input pixels. Lanczos-3 sinc-based kernel preserves sharpness better than bilinear or bicubic.

When to use

Universal safe choice. Best for text, screenshots, UI, charts, diagrams — anything where content must remain geometrically intact.

graphic Smart Crop Strong focal point
How it works

Divides the image into 16×16 tiles and computes the entropy (information density) of each. Uses a summed-area table to efficiently find the rectangular crop window that maximizes total entropy. The crop is then scaled to the exact target size.

When to use

Images with a strong focal point surrounded by uniform regions (e.g., a face in the center). Fails when important content is near the edges.

any Letterbox Aspect-ratio preservation
How it works

Scales the image to fit within the target dimensions while preserving aspect ratio, then fills the remaining area with a solid color (default black).

When to use

When aspect ratio must be preserved exactly. Common for video thumbnails, social media previews, and presentation slides.

default · photo Adaptive Hybrid General-purpose / photos (adaptive)
How it works

Two-stage retargeting (Rubinstein et al. 2009):

Stage 1 — Adaptive seam carving: Remove seams one at a time using forward-energy DP. Track a rolling median of seam energies. When the current seam's energy exceeds 2× the baseline, stop — the remaining content is too important to carve safely.

Stage 2 — Lanczos scale: The pixel deficit not handled by seam carving is covered with standard interpolation. This preserves content that would have been distorted by aggressive carving.

Key insight: The image itself decides the split. Uniform regions are carved away (zero cost), while dense content triggers early switching to scaling.

When to use

General-purpose resizing. Auto-classifier selects this for photos and default-class images.

Adaptive hybrid — detailed walkthrough

The hybrid algorithm is the most sophisticated approach. It combines seam carving with scaling and uses the image's own energy landscape to decide which pixels to carve and which to preserve. Here is exactly how it works.

Forward-energy seam carving (stage 1)

Traditional seam carving (Avidan & Shamir 2007) removes pixels with the lowest backward energy (gradient magnitude = how much the pixel differs from its neighbors). But this ignores a key fact: removing a pixel creates new edges between its former neighbors.

Forward energy (Rubinstein et al. 2008) fixes this. It precomputes the cost of the new edges that would appear after removal. For a pixel (i,j), it computes three costs depending on which direction the seam entered from above: 

M_LR = |I(i,j+1) - I(i,j-1)|           # new edge between left & right neighbors
M_LU = |I(i-1,j) - I(i,j-1)|           # extra cost if seam came from top-left
M_RU = |I(i-1,j) - I(i,j+1)|           # extra cost if seam came from top-right

CL = M_LR + M_LU   # cost when seam enters from top-left
CU = M_LR          # cost when seam enters straight from above
CR = M_LR + M_RU   # cost when seam enters from top-right

dp[i,j] = min(dp[i-1,j-1]+CL, dp[i-1,j]+CU, dp[i-1,j+1]+CR)

The DP finds the seam that introduces the least visible change to the image — not just the one through boring pixels.

Adaptive stopping criterion (the key innovation)

The algorithm tracks the forward energy of every seam it removes. After each seam, it computes a rolling median of non-zero seam energies (skipping zero-energy seams through uniform regions). When the current seam's energy exceeds 2× the median baseline, it stops carving immediately: 

nonzero = [e for e in seam_energies if e > 1e-6]
if len(nonzero) >= 5 and i >= 10:
    baseline = median(nonzero)            # median, not mean — robust to outliers
    if norm_e > baseline * 2.0:           # energy spiked — switch to scaling
        break

Using median instead of mean prevents a single expensive seam from raising the baseline. Skipping zero-energy seams avoids triggering the stop when the first uneven pixel is encountered in an otherwise uniform image.

Lanczos scaling (stage 2)

Any remaining pixel deficit not handled by seam carving is covered with Lanczos-3 interpolation. This preserves the geometric integrity of dense content (faces, text, edges) that the adaptive criterion identified as too expensive to carve.

Why this beats a fixed fraction

A fixed 70/30 split (carve 70%, scale 30%) applies the same policy to every image. The adaptive approach lets the image itself decide:

  • Photo with 60% uniform sky: Adaptive carves ~90% of the sky. Only stops when hitting trees. Fixed 70% would carve 70% indiscriminately and potentially cut into important ground content.
  • Text document with narrow margins: Adaptive carves ~10% (the white margins), then switches to scaling. Fixed 70% would carve too deep and distort letters.
  • Chart with wide borders: Adaptive carves all borders (zero cost), stops at the bars. Fixed 70% might carve into the bars and distort the data.

How the auto-classifier decides

FeaturePhotoGraphicText
Edge densitylowlow-midhigh
Color richnesshighlowlow
Selected algohybridscalescale

Run python resizer.py image.jpg --info to see per-image classification. The classifier examines three features at 256×256 resolution: edge density (>50% max gradient), normalized energy entropy, and approximate unique color ratio via random sampling (4096 pixels).

Adaptive hybrid decisions

Format: carved + scaled (width+height)

ImageSeams carvedScaled
Photo400 + 150 + 285
Text96 + 102304 + 198
Chart234 + 300166 + 0
UI144 + 210256 + 90
Graphic365 + 30035 + 0

Seams are removed from low-energy regions first. The algorithm switches to scaling as soon as seam energy spikes above 2× the baseline median, protecting important content from distortion.

Based on Avidan & Shamir 2007, Rubinstein et al. 2008 & 2009 — 5 image types, 30 comparisons
